section.title: Water into Wine and the Temple
section.sectionRationale: John pairs two opening scenes that announce the new order Jesus brings. At Cana He turns water for ritual washing into the best wine, the first of His signs, revealing His glory. In Jerusalem He cleanses the temple and points to the true temple, His own body, to be destroyed and raised in three days. Old vessels and old temple give way to the joy and presence Jesus Himself embodies.
section.thesis: In His first sign Jesus transforms the water of ritual purification into the abundant wine of the messianic feast, revealing His glory, and in cleansing the temple He announces that the true meeting place with God is His own body, to be destroyed and raised, so that both scenes proclaim a new order centered on Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the wedding at Cana where water becomes wine and glory is revealed; the cleansing of the temple and the demand for a sign; and the riddle of the temple of His body, with a closing note that Jesus knew what was in every heart.

units.label: The wedding at Cana
units.note: the first sign, His glory revealed

verses.text: On the third day a wedding took place at Cana in Galilee. Jesus’ mother was there,
and Jesus and His disciples had also been invited to the wedding.
When the wine ran out, Jesus’ mother said to Him, “They have no more wine.”
“Woman, what is that to you and to Me?” Jesus replied. “My {{hora|hour}} has not yet come.”
His mother said to the servants, “Do whatever He tells you.”
Now six stone water jars had been set there for the Jewish rites of purification. Each could hold from twenty to thirty gallons.
Jesus told the servants, “Fill the jars with water.” So they filled them to the brim.
“Now draw some out,” He said, “and take it to the master of the banquet.” They did so,
and the master of the banquet tasted the water that had been turned into wine. He did not know where it was from, but the servants who had drawn the water knew. Then he called the bridegroom aside
and said, “Everyone serves the fine wine first, and then the cheap wine after the guests are drunk. But you have saved the fine wine until now!”
Jesus performed this, the first of His {{semeion|signs}}, at Cana in Galilee. He thus revealed His {{doxa|glory}}, and His disciples believed in Him.

units.label: Cleansing the temple
units.note: My Father's house is not a marketplace

verses.text: After this, He went down to Capernaum with His mother and brothers and His disciples, and they stayed there a few days.
When the Jewish Passover was near, Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
In the temple courts He found men selling cattle, sheep, and doves, and money changers seated at their tables.
So He made a whip out of cords and drove all from the temple courts, both sheep and cattle. He poured out the coins of the money changers and overturned their tables.
To those selling doves He said, “Get these out of here! How dare you turn My Father’s house into a marketplace!”
His disciples remembered that it is written: “Zeal for Your house will consume Me.”

units.label: The temple of His body
units.note: destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up

verses.text: On account of this, the Jews demanded, “What sign can You show us to prove Your authority to do these things?”
Jesus answered, “Destroy this {{naos|temple}}, and in three days I will raise it up again.”
“This temple took forty-six years to build,” the Jews replied, “and You are going to raise it up in three days?”
But Jesus was speaking about the temple of His body.
After He was raised from the dead, His disciples remembered that He had said this. Then they believed the Scripture and the word that Jesus had spoken.
While He was in Jerusalem at the Passover Feast, many people saw the signs He was doing and believed in His name.
But Jesus did not entrust Himself to them, for He knew them all.
He did not need any testimony about man, for He knew what was in a man.

hora.gloss: Hour, appointed time. In John the hour is a technical term for the appointed moment of Jesus' death and glorification. At Cana He says His hour has not yet come; through the Gospel the hour approaches, until in the upper room He declares that the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified. Everything moves toward that hour at the cross.

semeion.gloss: Sign. John does not call Jesus' miracles wonders but signs, because each one points beyond itself to who Jesus is and to the life He gives. The Gospel is structured around seven such signs, beginning here at Cana. A sign is meant to be read, so that seeing the act leads to believing in the person and receiving life.

doxa.gloss: Glory, the visible weight and splendor of God's presence. The prologue said the disciples beheld His glory; the first sign is where that glory begins to be revealed in action, and His disciples believed. In John this glory shines most fully at the cross, the hour when the Son is glorified.

naos.gloss: Temple, sanctuary, the dwelling place of God. Jesus takes the word for the sacred building and applies it to His own body: destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up. The true meeting place between God and humanity is no longer a structure of stone but the crucified and risen Christ, in whom God dwells.

basic.context: John places two scenes side by side to show the new thing Jesus brings. At a village wedding the wine runs out, a social disaster, and Jesus quietly turns roughly a hundred and twenty gallons of washing water into the finest wine. John calls it the first of His signs and says it revealed His glory. Then, in Jerusalem at Passover, Jesus drives the traders from the temple and, challenged for a sign, speaks a riddle: destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up. He meant His body.
basic.hermeneutics: The key is the word sign. These are not just miracles to amaze; they are pictures pointing to who Jesus is. The water for ritual washing becoming abundant wine hints that Jesus brings the joy and fullness the old rituals only anticipated. The temple scene raises the question, where do we now meet God? John answers, in the crucified and risen body of Jesus. Notice too that the disciples only understood the temple saying after the resurrection.
basic.originalLanguages: Two words carry the section. Jesus says His hora, His hour, has not yet come, the appointed time of His death and glory that the whole Gospel moves toward. And John names the miracle the first of His semeion, His signs, by which He revealed His doxa, His glory. In Jerusalem He redefines the naos, the temple, as His own body.
basic.typology: The old jars of purification water, replaced by wine, picture the old covenant order giving way to the joy of the messianic feast the prophets foretold. The temple of stone gives way to the true temple, the body of Christ, where God and humanity finally meet, destroyed at the cross and raised on the third day.
basic.christ: Both signs are about Christ. He is the true bridegroom who provides the best wine, the host of the feast of the age to come. He is the true temple, the place of God's presence, torn down in death and raised in three days. He knows every heart and needs no one to tell Him what is in a person.
basic.god: God is a God of abundant joy, not stingy religion; the wine is lavish and the best is saved for last. He is also jealous for true worship, so that His house is not made a marketplace. And He is present, no longer chiefly in a building but in His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 25 and Amos 9 promise a feast of rich wine in the age to come. Psalm 69 supplies zeal for Your house. Revelation 21 shows the new creation where God dwells with His people and there is no temple but God and the Lamb.
basic.oneStory: The story is turning from shadow to substance. The washing rites and the temple pointed forward, and now the reality has come. Jesus brings the abundant wine of the promised feast and becomes Himself the true temple, the meeting place of God and humanity, opened to all through His death and resurrection.

context.literary: The first two public scenes of Jesus' ministry, deliberately paired: a private sign in Galilee that reveals glory to disciples, and a public confrontation in Jerusalem that provokes the authorities. Both turn on the theme of newness replacing the old, and both look ahead, one to the hour, the other to the resurrection. John frames them with the counting of days, on the third day at Cana, and closes with a note on Jesus' knowledge of the human heart.
context.historical: Weddings were multi-day village celebrations where running out of wine would shame the host's family; hospitality was a sacred duty. The stone jars held water for ceremonial washing before meals. In Jerusalem, the temple courts hosted the sale of approved sacrificial animals and the exchange of currency into the temple shekel; the trade had encroached on the court of the Gentiles. Herod's temple had been under construction about forty-six years.
context.canonical: The Synoptics place a temple cleansing in Passion week; John sets a cleansing at the outset, framing Jesus' whole ministry as the coming of the true temple. The sign at Cana inaugurates the Book of Signs. The promise to raise the temple in three days links to the resurrection and to Old Testament hopes of a purified worship, and it anticipates the New Testament teaching that Christ and His people are the temple of God, 1 Corinthians 3:16 and Ephesians 2:21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To open the Book of Signs by showing that Jesus brings the abundant new order the old covenant anticipated, revealing His glory in the first sign, and to reveal Him as the true temple, the risen place of meeting between God and humanity.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes particular events, a wedding and a temple cleansing, not a rule for weddings or a program for reform. What abides is who Jesus is shown to be: the bringer of messianic joy and the true temple, and the fitting response of believing in Him.

debates.question: Why does Jesus address His mother as Woman and say My hour has not yet come, yet then act?

views.label: A gentle distancing of authority
views.case: Jesus signals that His mission runs on the Father's timetable, not family pressure; Woman is respectful but marks that His works flow from His hour, not human prompting, even as He graciously provides.

views.label: A veiled pointer to the cross
views.case: The hour in John is the cross and glorification; the reply hints that the true wine of the feast will finally be poured out then, so the sign is a foretaste of a glory not yet fully come.

debates.bearing: Either way the sign is tied to the hour: the abundance at Cana is a preview of the greater provision Jesus will make when His hour comes at the cross.

debates.question: Is John's temple cleansing the same event as the one in the Synoptic Gospels' Passion week?

views.label: The same event, placed thematically
views.case: Some hold there was one cleansing in Passion week, and John moves it to the front for theological reasons, to frame Jesus' ministry as the coming of the true temple.

views.label: Two distinct cleansings
views.case: Others hold Jesus cleansed the temple twice, once early as John records and once late as the Synoptics record; the differing details and timing suggest separate occasions.

debates.bearing: Either way the meaning is the same: Jesus claims authority over God's house and points beyond it to the true temple, His own body, raised on the third day.

study.originalLanguages: The section is threaded with key Johannine terms. At Cana Jesus says His hora, His hour, has not yet come, the appointed time of His death and glory to which the whole Gospel presses. John calls the miracle the first of the semeion, the signs, deeds that point beyond themselves, and says Jesus thereby revealed His doxa, His glory, so that the disciples believed. In Jerusalem Jesus takes the word naos, the temple sanctuary, God's dwelling, and applies it to His own body, destroyed and raised in three days, so that the true meeting place with God is now the crucified and risen Christ.

typology.type: The water of ritual purification turned to abundant wine
typology.fulfillment: The old covenant order of washing fulfilled in the joy and fullness of the messianic feast Jesus brings, Isaiah 25:6.
The true temple, the body of Christ, destroyed at the cross and raised on the third day, in whom God dwells, John 2:21 and Ephesians 2:21.

typology.type: The third day at Cana and the raising in three days
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection of Jesus on the third day, the ground of the disciples' later understanding and faith.

study.christ: Christ is revealed as the true bridegroom and host who provides the best wine, the abundance of the age to come, in His very first sign. He is the temple in person, the place where God dwells and is met, so that when the authorities demand a sign He points to His own death and resurrection, destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up. He acts with divine authority in His Father's house, and He possesses divine knowledge, needing no one to tell Him what is in a person, for He knows every human heart.
study.god: God is shown to be lavishly generous, turning a social embarrassment into a feast of the finest wine, a picture of the joy He delights to give. He is also holy and jealous for true worship, refusing to let His house be turned into a marketplace, so that zeal for His house consumes His Son. And He is the God who has drawn near, no longer dwelling chiefly in a building but in the body of His Son, and soon by His Spirit in His people.

crossReferences.note: The LORD will make a feast of rich food and well-aged wine for all peoples, the messianic banquet foreshadowed at Cana.
Zeal for Your house has consumed me, the text the disciples recall at the temple cleansing.
You are God's temple and His Spirit dwells in you, unfolding the new temple reality Jesus inaugurates.
In the new Jerusalem there is no temple, for the Lord God and the Lamb are its temple, the consummation of this theme.

study.oneStory: The whole story is moving from shadow to reality. The rites of washing and the temple of stone were always pointers, and now the substance has come. Jesus brings the abundant wine of the promised feast and reveals Himself as the true temple, the meeting place of God and humanity, to be torn down at the cross and raised on the third day. From here the Gospel unfolds sign after sign, each one revealing His glory and inviting belief that leads to life.

quiz.q: Why does John call the turning of water into wine the first of Jesus' signs?

options.text: Because it points beyond itself to who Jesus is and reveals His glory, leading the disciples to believe.
options.note: Verse 11 states the sign revealed His glory and led to faith; signs are meant to be read.

options.text: Because it was the most impressive miracle He ever did.
options.note: John's point is not scale but meaning; a sign points to Jesus' identity, not merely amazes.

options.text: Because it showed His power and provided joy at the feast.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the deeper point is that the sign reveals His glory and calls forth belief.

options.text: Because it proved He approved of every celebration.
options.note: The sign is about revealing Jesus' glory, not a general endorsement of parties.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up?

options.text: He is speaking of the temple of His body, His death and resurrection on the third day.
options.note: Verse 21 explains He spoke of His body; the true temple is the risen Christ.

options.text: He is promising to rebuild Herod's temple supernaturally.
options.note: Verse 21 rules this out; He was not speaking of the stone building.

options.text: He is pointing to the resurrection as the sign of His authority.
options.note: True that His resurrection is the sign, but the answer omits that the temple He means is His own body.

options.text: He is predicting the temple's future destruction by Rome.
options.note: That destruction did come later, but here Jesus speaks of His body, not the Roman siege.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the water jars being for the Jewish rites of purification?

options.text: Jesus transforms the water of the old ritual order into the abundant wine of the messianic feast.
options.note: The detail signals the old order giving way to the joy and fullness Jesus brings.

options.text: It shows Jesus disregarded Jewish law entirely.
options.note: Jesus fulfills rather than despises the law; He brings what the rites anticipated.

options.text: It shows Jesus provided a large quantity of wine.
options.note: The quantity is real, but the point is the purification jars becoming the vessels of new joy.

options.text: It hints that Jesus brings something greater than the old washings.
options.note: Right direction, but it stops short of the specific picture of the messianic feast of wine.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus not entrust Himself to those who believed because of His signs, verses 23 to 25?

options.text: Because He knew every heart and knew their belief was shallow.
options.note: Verses 24 and 25 say He knew what was in each person; He was not deceived by sign-based enthusiasm.

options.text: Because He disliked crowds.
options.note: The reason given is His knowledge of hearts, not any dislike of people.

options.text: Because belief based only on signs can be unreliable.
options.note: True and implied, but the text grounds it specifically in Jesus' knowledge of what was in each person.

options.text: Because He wanted to keep His identity secret a while longer.
options.note: There is a theme of the hour, but the stated reason is His knowledge of their hearts, not secrecy.

quiz.q: What does Jesus reveal about God in cleansing the temple?

options.text: That God is jealous for true worship and will not have His house made a marketplace.
options.note: Verse 16 and the zeal of Psalm 69 show God's holy concern for pure worship.

options.text: That God is indifferent to how He is worshiped.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus' zeal shows God cares intensely about the purity of worship.

options.text: That God opposes all buying and selling anywhere.
options.note: The issue is corrupting His house, not commerce in general.

options.text: That God's house is meant for prayer and His presence, not profit.
options.note: True and close, but it softens the sharp note of God's jealous zeal for His house.

questions: At Cana Jesus saved the best wine for last. Where are you tempted to think the joy God gives is thin or second-rate, when He means to give His finest?
Mary told the servants, do whatever He tells you. What is Jesus telling you to do that you have been hesitant to obey?
Jesus was consumed with zeal for His Father's house. What has crept into your worship of God that needs to be driven out?
The disciples only understood the temple saying after the resurrection. Where might you need to trust Jesus' words now and wait for understanding to come later?
Jesus knew what was in every heart and was not fooled by shallow enthusiasm. How does it change your walk with Him to know that He sees you completely and still calls you His own?

translationNotes.note: Twenty to thirty gallons renders the Greek measure; the BSB footnote gives two or three metretae, about 20.8 to 31.2 gallons or 78.8 to 118.1 liters each, so the six jars held a lavish quantity.
Temple courts renders a word the BSB footnote notes is literally the temple, also in verse 15; the trade filled the outer courts, the court of the Gentiles.
Zeal for Your house will consume Me quotes Psalm 69:9, a psalm of the righteous sufferer that the disciples saw fulfilled in Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 2:1-25. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Forty-six in verse 20 retains its ordinary hyphen, which is not an em or en dash. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
